Name: layerwise_information_compression
Title: Layerwise Information Compression: An Information-Theoretic Approach to Neural Network Pruning
Short Hypothesis: Different layers of neural networks contain varying amounts of task-relevant information; strategically pruning layers based on their information content can optimize model compression while maintaining performance.
Related Work: Existing works have explored model compression through pruning and quantization from an information-theoretic standpoint, but few have explicitly connected layer-wise information content to compression strategies. Notable works include the derivation of trade-offs between compression and performance (Isik et al., 2021) and the analysis of generalization risk with model compression (Bu et al., 2019). This proposal differentiates itself by focusing on a systematic method to assess and utilize the information content of individual layers for targeted compression.
Abstract: In this work, we propose an innovative framework for neural network pruning grounded in information theory. We hypothesize that different network layers encapsulate varying degrees of task-relevant information and that quantifying this information can guide effective model compression strategies. By leveraging mutual information and entropy measures, we aim to develop a layerwise information assessment metric that quantifies the contribution of each layer to the overall model performance. Based on this metric, we will introduce a pruning strategy that selectively removes parameters from layers with lower information content, thereby optimizing the trade-off between model size and task performance. We will validate our approach through theoretical bounds on achievable rate-distortion curves and empirical evaluations on benchmark datasets, demonstrating the effectiveness of our method in achieving state-of-the-art compression rates without sacrificing predictive accuracy.
Experiments: Develop an information estimator using mutual information and entropy to quantify task-relevant information per layer in various neural network architectures.
Implement a pruning algorithm that utilizes the layer information estimates to selectively prune weights from layers with lower information content.
Evaluate the performance of the pruned models on benchmark datasets such as CIFAR-10 and ImageNet, measuring accuracy, model size, and compression rate.
Compare the proposed method against existing pruning strategies to assess improvements in compression efficiency and predictive performance.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The accuracy of the information estimators may vary based on the architecture and dataset, potentially influencing pruning decisions.
Layerwise information content may not always correlate perfectly with task performance, leading to suboptimal pruning outcomes.
The proposed framework may require tuning of parameters related to information estimation and pruning thresholds, which could introduce variability.
